title: Classic Vegetable Risotto

subs.name: regular Indian short-grain rice (sona masoori) or basmati
subs.notes: basmati gives a slightly different, less starchy texture; keep stirring to release starch

subs.name: 2 vegetable stock cubes dissolved in 1.2 l hot water
subs.notes: Maggi or Knorr vegetable stock cubes are widely available in India

subs.name: extra olive oil or a vegan butter spread
subs.notes: butter is crucial for the creamy finish; use a good substitute for best results

subs.name: 3 tbsp nutritional yeast + a splash of coconut cream for a vegan version

steps.text: Keep the stock warm in a small saucepan over low heat throughout cooking — cold stock stops the cooking process and makes a gluey risotto.
Heat half the butter and all the olive oil in a wide, heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 4–5 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the garlic and fry for 1 minute.
Add the rice and stir it around in the butter for 2 minutes until each grain turns opaque at the centre — this toasting step stops the risotto from becoming mushy.

Add the first ladle of warm stock and stir until it is almost fully absorbed. Continue adding stock one ladle at a time, stirring continuously and waiting for absorption before adding the next. This will take 18–20 minutes.

After about 15 minutes, stir in the peas and zucchini with the next ladle of stock. Continue stirring until the rice is tender but still has a slight bite at the centre and the overall consistency is flowing, not stiff.

Remove the pan from heat. Add the remaining butter and all the grated Parmesan and stir vigorously (this is called mantecatura) until the risotto turns glossy and creamy. Squeeze in lemon juice, check salt and pepper.
Serve immediately in warm bowls — risotto sets quickly and does not reheat well. Top with extra Parmesan and black pepper.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice → regular Indian short-grain rice (sona masoori) or basmati (basmati gives a slightly different, less starchy texture; keep stirring to release starch); cheese → 3 tbsp nutritional yeast + a splash of coconut cream for a vegan version (adds cheesy depth without dairy); butter → extra olive oil or a vegan butter spread (butter is crucial for the creamy finish; use a good substitute for best results); stock → 2 vegetable stock cubes dissolved in 1.2 l hot water (Maggi or Knorr vegetable stock cubes are widely available in India)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A heavy-bottomed kadai or a deep non-stick pan works perfectly. Use Indian sona masoori or a short-grain rice — basmati also works but give it more stirring to release starch. Vegetable stock cubes from Knorr or Maggi dissolved in hot water are an easy shortcut. The pressure-cooker shortcut: cook the soffritto (onion-garlic) first in a kadai, then transfer everything with all the stock to a pressure cooker, seal and cook for 2 whistles on medium heat. Release pressure naturally, then stir in butter and cheese — you lose some texture but save 15 minutes.
